texto: Do those scars cover the whole of you, like the stars and the moons on your dess? I though that would be pretty too, and I ask you right here please to agree with me that a scar is never ugly. Because take it from me, a scar does not form on dying. A scar means, I survivied.

texto: One didn't understand, until one had seen a great many bodies, the unconscious effort that one must be making every minute simply to keep one's hands and face and clothes clean. texto: On the girl's brown legs there were many small white scars. I was thinking, Do those scars cover the whole of you, like the stars and the moons on your dress? I thought that would be pretty too, and I ask you right here please to agree with me that a scar is never ugly. That is what the scar makers want us to think. But you and I, we must make an agreement to defy them. We must see all scars as beauty. Okay? This will be our secret. Because take it from me, a scar does not form on the dying. A scar means, I survived.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Chris Cleave: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de limites e escolhas conscientes. O trecho “On the girl's brown legs there were many small white scars. I was thinking, Do those scars cover …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, healing, scars pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de scars.

texto: Sorry. But it isn’t for us to change how things are. I’m just an administrator. You’re just a teacher.”“Oh, I hope I don’t teach.
